https://github.com/godotengine/godot/com.commit_message:
Merge pull request #114325 from KoBeWi/juggling_paths_editor_edition

Ensure scene paths in autoload settings

description.text:
This PR makes sure that any scene that is loaded through autoload is initialized and cached first, then it loads the scene.

This makes it so that `GDScriptCache::get_packed_scene()` can retreive that scene even if it's in the middle of its parsing.

Fixes #69073 where children nodes would try to load a new instance of the singleton, where chaos ensued.

### Godot version

v4.0.beta6.official [7f8ecffa5]

### System information

Windows 10

### Issue description

When using a global auto-loaded node, the editor will delete the children of the node if they are referenced via a script.

This occurred in my project that is stable in Godot 4 beta 4 and I noticed right away that my one global node had most of the children deleted when I opened the project in Godot 4 beta 6.

I was able to recreate a minimum project with ease by setting up an auto-loaded node that has children and scripts that reference the global name.

I build the autoloaded node like this with two children:
![](https://jeffgamedev.com/img/dev/singleton-issue.png?raw=true)

Here is the raw text of the node:
![](https://jeffgamedev.com/img/dev/singleton-issue-raw.png?raw=true)

After reloading the project, the editor has deleted my child nodes:
![](https://jeffgamedev.com/img/dev/empty-global-raw.png)

This is a major regression from Godot 4 beta 1, 2, 3, and 4.


### Steps to reproduce

1. Create a scene with a corresponding script and add it to as an auto-loaded global.
2. Create two child nodes with corresponding scripts and add them as children to the auto-loaded node. Create onready references to them in the auto-loaded node's script.
    ![](https://jeffgamedev.com/img/dev/singleton-issue.png?raw=true)
3. In one of the child node scripts, reference the other child of the auto-loaded node from the global reference.
    ![](https://jeffgamedev.com/img/dev/singleton-issue-child-b.png?raw=true)
5. Click Project > Reload Current Project
6. Open the auto-loaded node and observe that the editor has deleted it's child nodes.
    ![](https://jeffgamedev.com/img/dev/singleton-deleted-children.png?raw=true)

### Minimal reproduction project

[editor-deletes-nodes.zip](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/files/10079156/editor-deletes-nodes.zip)

Attached is my minimum reproduction project. When you open the project, the editor will delete the child nodes before you can observe in the scene hierarchy. To prove that it is opening, I recommend opening the `singleton.tscn` node in an external editor, then opening in editor to observe that the nodes have been deleted. You can also re-add the child nodes, `map.tscn` and `ui.tscn`, and all appears well until you do the step I describe above to Reload the project, and you can observe that once again the children have been deleted.
